data.projectDetailsTitle: Artificial Intelligence for Label Review: FertiScan
data.projectDetailsDescription:
The Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) is responsible for safeguarding food, animal, and plant health, which includes overseeing labeling to ensure accuracy and compliance with Canadian laws and regulations. At a high level, label review at CFIA is split into two streams of work:

•	Marketplace Monitoring: reviewing labels of products available for sale in the Canadian marketplace
•	New Product Registration (Fertilizer and Animal Feed): label review is one aspect contained in a detailed package sent to CFIA representatives to obtain a registration prior to sale within the Canadian Marketplace – a requirement for various fertilizer and animal feed products. 

At this time, primary products which must adhere to CFIA labelling requirements include:

•	Food
•	Animal Feed
•	Fertilizer and supplements

Below is a brief summary of some of the pre-market label review activities:

•	Food: Falls out of scope – no registration is required. However, pre-market label review is voluntary. While the CFIA does not mandate pre-market approval for most food labels, it offers a voluntary label review service for industry stakeholders seeking guidance on compliance
•	Animal Feed: Review of labels for new registration of animal feed products is a critical step in ensuring that these products meet regulatory requirements for safety, efficacy, and transparency. Proofs of labels are included in packages submitted to the animal feed registration office, the CFIA then reviews the proposed feed label for compliance with the Feeds Act and Regulations. 
•	Fertilizer: Similar to Animal Feed, some fertilizer products require a registration. Prior to sale in the Canadian Marketplace, a detailed package is submitted to the Fertilizer Safety Office, and included is the proposed label. In addition to toxicological assessment, the Fertilizer Safety Office reviews the label to ensure compliance with regulatory requirements. 

Over the last fiscal year, CFIA representatives across the Plant Health Business Line, and Digital Services Branch have collaborated on the development and assessment of an Artificial Intelligence prototype which could be used to assist with various label review activities. The focus to-date has been on the development of a tool which can extract key details from a fertilizer product (Plant Health) label. Throughout the project, colleagues from the Animal Health and Food Safety Business Lines have also been consulted and have expressed interest in a similar tool.
data.businessDrivers3:
Through consultation/current state assessment with the CFIA's Operations Branch, and Policy and Programs Branch, the following gaps were identified when it comes to label review across food products, animal feed and fertilizers:

•	Quotas for operational label verification efforts across all three business lines are not being met, risking an increase in non-compliant products available across the marketplace
•	Label review across all three business lines involves a significant amount of manual effort and administrative burden. This, combined with an increased prioritization of on-demand activities such as export related work over planned activities (i.e. market place monitoring such as label review) makes completing label review activities difficult. With operational resources experiencing strain across the country, CFIA has the opportunity to focus on investing in tools which help streamline processes, eliminate administrative burden, and alleviate strain on Agency resources. 
•	Service Standards are worsening, and backlogs are increasing for new product registrations for both fertilizer and animal feed, limiting industry’s ability to bring new products to market and risking a decrease in Canada’s international market share, negatively affecting key sectors that contribute to Canada’s economic well being
                     o	Canada is one of the top 3 exporters of fertilizer across global markets, with the industry contributing $23B to Canda’s GDP in 2023

After identifying the gaps noted above, the following needs were identified:

-Extraction of Text Off Label Images: Right now, inspectors or evaluators in CFIA's registration offices must complete a thorough review of key data fields on a label and complete a lengthy checklist to determine if the label is in compliance. A tool is needed to efficiently extract key fields of data off a label for more efficient review. The AI prototype currently developed meets this need by using optical character recognition and quickly extracting key data into a user friendly dashboard

-Centralized storage of previously reviewed labels: Right now, label review records are stored in a decentralized and often unorganized manner, making it difficult and time consuming for CFIA employees to search for past labels reviewed and leading to potential duplication of effort, where the same product label is inspected multiple times by different inspectors across the country. A centralized data storage solution which can be used to search for past label review efforts, or flag if a product has already been reviewed would eliminate duplication of efforts and save time during label review and marketplace monitoring activities. The system would meet this need by organizing all records of previous labels reviewed, and if possible, automatically flag to users if a label has already been reviewed.

-Automatic flagging of potential areas of non compliance for CFIA review: Right now, inspectors or evaluators in registration offices must thoroughly analyze labels and compare what is presented on each label with compliance standards noted in CFIA acts, regulations, policies and standard operating procedures. It would be beneficial if the solution could automatically flag potential areas of non compliance. This need would be met by training the algorithm to reference appropriate acts/regulations/policies and compare them with what is presented on the label. 

-Comparative review and flagging of differences: One activity conducted for fertilizer and animal feed registration renewals is the comparison of previously submitted labels with what is included in the renewal submission package. Differences are noted and assessed for compliance. It would be beneficial for the tool to be able to efficiently flag potential differences for CFIA review. This would require training the tool to be able to identify and flag the differences.
data.businessDrivers4:
Positive public benefits could include:

-Economic Benefits: Adding efficiency to the process of obtaining new registrations for products would help more Canadian products become eligible for sale domestically and abroad and greater speeds, positively contributing to Canadian jobs and the country's GDP

-Increased compliance: increasing CFIA's ability to conduct label review activities would inevitably lead to less products on the market which have non compliant labels
data.businessDrivers6:
Efforts in fiscal year 2024-2025 have led to the development of a strong proof of concept / prototype that can take an image of a fertilizer label and extract 62 key fields of data in a user-friendly dashboard for inspector review. This sets the foundation for a tool that can be enhanced with more automation features, and expanded upon to support all three of the Agency’s business lines.

In order to fully capitalize on potential benefits, further investment is required to:

•	Customize the label review tool to meet the requirements of the food and animal business lines
•	Establish “If-Then” relationships within the A.I algorithm to be able to efficiently and automatically flag areas of potential non-compliance for inspector review and for training purposes
•	Enhance features within the tool, for example, expanding capabilities to allow for the comparison of newly submitted labels for product registration renewals/amendments with previously submitted labels, which would increase efficiency when it comes to fertilizer and feed registration activities.  

Should further investment in and development of FertiScan occur, end-state benefits include:

•	Efficiency gains: Currently, due to manual processes and administrative burden, label review across all three business lines can take up to 4 hours. It is anticipated that A.I could reduce this by more than 50%.
•	Elimination of duplication of effort: Having the tool capable of flagging whether or not a product label has already been reviewed would eliminate circumstances where the same label is reviewed multiple times across the country. 
•	Reduction of Backlogs for Registration Offices: For products which require a lengthy registration review process such as Animal Feed and Fertilizer, a label review tool could potentially reduce review time required and as a result, lessen the current backlogs in requests. 
•	Multipurposed foundation: This tool leverages the use of Optical Character Recognition, capable of taking an image and extracting text accurately. Investment in a label review tool would allow the Agency to have the foundational technology required to adapt or expand for alternative purposes. 
•	Applicable to all three business lines: A label review tool would serve all three of the Agency’s business lines.
data.businessDrivers7: The tool is only trained using CFIA data for very specific purposes.
data.businessDrivers8: Industry interests generally circulate around obtaining permissions as quick as possible, while program objectives aim to ensure that all labels on food, animal feed and fertilizer products are compliant with applicable acts and regulations. The tool would balance efficiency with the need to ensure that it can accurately support the identification of non compliance in product labels.
data.businessDrivers10: CFIA has been manually reviews labels since the fertilizer, food and animal feed programs were established. Current processes involve administrative burden and the completion of tedious check lists. Automation where possible is preferred as it will allow CFIA resources to conduct their work more accurately and efficiently, and alleviate the current resource strain experienced across programs.
data.decisionSector3: The tool will be used to automate the extraction of text off of product labels and help identify potential areas of non-compliance for CFIA representative review
data.impact31: The system would be used to extract data fields from product labels and potentially flag areas which may be non-compliant. CFIA representatives would then review to make the final decision/verification as to whether or not the product label is non compliant.
data.impact20: CFIA standard operating procedures, checklists and applicable policies/regulations are used to train the model to be able to extract key fields of data and using OCR, the model pulls the necessary information into a user dashboard.
data.impact21: The tool currently extracts key fields of data from a fertilizer label and presents it to the user on a series of carousel screens that are organized by section (i.e. 1. company information, 2. cautionary statements 3. ingredients 4. composition etc). The user is asked to validate the information. The image of the label is also included for reference.
data.impact8:
When the CFIA finds a product label that doesn’t meet Canadian regulatory requirements, CFIA conducts the following activities:

1. Assessment of Risk and Severity – CFIA inspectors first evaluate how serious the labeling issue is. Factors include whether the mislabeling could confuse consumers, misrepresent the product, or pose a health risk (for example, by failing to declare allergens).

2. Notification and Corrective Action – In many cases, the CFIA will notify the manufacturer or importer of the specific shortcomings of the label. They’ll typically be given a set period to correct the issue. This might mean redesigning or updating the label to ensure it complies with the applicable standards under legislation such as the Food and Drugs Act and the Consumer Packaging and Labelling Act.

3. Enforcement Actions – If the company doesn’t respond adequately, or if the label’s deficiencies are deemed high risk (such as misleading claims or failing to list allergens), the CFIA may take more direct enforcement actions. These can include:
 • Ordering the product’s removal from the market.
 • Conducting or mandating a product recall if the label could endanger consumer health.
 • Seizing products at the border or from retail outlets if they’re found to be non-compliant.
 • Issuing warning letters, administrative monetary penalties, or pursuing legal action for repeated or particularly egregious offenses.

4.Public Communication – In cases where public health could be compromised, the CFIA may also choose to inform consumers about the issue, either through press releases or public advisories.
data.impact10: no impact to the rights or freedoms of individuals
data.impact25: no impact on equality, dignity, privacy or autonomy of individuals
data.impact12:
For food: By effectively identifying products which are mislabeled, CFIA ensures that food products available to Canadians do not present potential allergens unless they are labeled as such. 

For Fertilizer products: instructions and cautionary statements must be present. The tool would help improve label review activities that aim to ensure this. This would support the health/well-being of those using the fertilizer products
data.impact14: Should a label be deemed as non compliant, it is possible that the product would be pulled from the marketplace, affecting the sales of the company with the non compliant labelled product.
data.privacyDesign5: A privacy impact assessment is currently underway through a wider effort assess various digital tools at CFIA
data.privacyDesign8: Only inspectors have access to their inspector user name etc. no other personal information is captured.
